fact: A layer of warm air sealed the city like a lid — a temperature inversion trapped smoke over London
source: Met Office — The Great Smog of 1952
UK Met Office and contemporary reports documented visibility dropping to a few yards or less; one-foot visibility is cited in popular accounts. See: 'London's Great Smog,' BBC History

fact: Sulfur dioxide mixes with moisture and becomes acid in your lungs
source: Bell, M.L., Davis, D.L., & Fletcher, T. 'A Retrospective Assessment of Mortality from the London Smog Episode of 1952.' Environmental Health Perspectives, 112(1), 2004

fact: Twelve thousand of your neighbors don't survive
source: Bell, M.L. & Davis, D.L. 'Reassessment of the Lethal London Fog of 1952: Novel Indicators of Acute and Chronic Consequences of Acute Exposure to Air Pollution.' Environmental Health Perspectives, 109(suppl 3), 2001 — estimated ~12,000 excess deaths

fact: The government took four years to ban the coal that killed them — the Clean Air Act was passed in 1956
